topic: A person learns their apartment building is going condo
raw_output:
[story 1]

The mailman dropped the stack of envelopes with a dull thud onto my worn welcome mat. I picked them up, my fingers tracing the embossed letterhead with a shaky grip. The subject line, stark and unyielding, read: "Notice of Condominium Conversion." My breath hitched. I reread the fine print, the jargon swimming before my eyes—'mandatory resale covenants,' 'HOA assessments,' 'unit ownership.' It all coalesced into a single, cold dread pooling in my stomach. I stood by the window, staring down at the familiar brick facade of the building, suddenly seeing it not as a home, but as a collection of assets being liquidated. My hands started to tremble, and I had to press them flat against the cool glass to steady myself.

[story 2]

I found the official notification taped to the lobby bulletin board. The paper felt flimsy, almost insulting in its casual presentation of something so monumental. Every word seemed to vibrate with finality. I paced the length of my living room three times, my footsteps echoing too loudly in the quiet apartment. I stopped by the kitchen counter, gripping the edge until my knuckles were white. I looked around the space—the familiar mismatched furniture, the slightly faded paint—and suddenly everything felt temporary, provisional. A tight knot tightened beneath my ribs, making it hard to draw a full breath. I sank onto the sofa, pulling my knees to my chest, wishing the floor would just swallow me whole.

[story 3]

It happened during my evening walk past the building. The signage, previously unremarkable, now featured a sleek, modern logo. I slowed my pace, my eyes scanning the details, searching for the source of this unsettling shift. When I finally reached the ground floor, I spotted the posted flyers. My heart began to hammer against my ribs, a frantic, uneven rhythm. I bent down, my movements stiff, and picked up the document. The sheer weight of the information settled heavily on my shoulders. I stood there for a long moment, the sounds of the city fading into a distant hum, my jaw clenched so tightly I could feel the strain in my neck muscles. I needed to process this, but the words just blurred into an overwhelming pressure behind my eyes.
